mnemonics.title: User Research Methods
mnemonics.content: I S C O F A D (Interview, Survey, Card Sorting, Observation, Focus group, Affinity diagram, Diary study)

mnemonics.title: Qualitative vs Quantitative
mnemonics.content: Q - Questions (open-ended), Observations; Q - Numbers (closed questions), Counts

mnemonics.title: Persona Elements
mnemonics.content: P.E.R.S.O.N.A (Photo, Emotions, Role, Scenario, Objectives, Needs, Attributes)

mnemonics.title: Journey Map Components
mnemonics.content: S.T.E.P.S (Stages, Touchpoints, Emotions, Pain points, Solutions)

acronyms.usage: Metrics used to measure success of design decisions and user experience
Business metric to justify design investments to stakeholders
Customer loyalty metric derived from surveys
Structure and organization of content, often tested with card sorting
Overall experience of users interacting with a product
The visual and interactive elements users interact with

visualTips.visualization: Imagine physical index cards scattered on a table being grouped into piles - each pile represents a category in user's mind
Think of a roller coaster line drawing - ups and downs show emotional highs and lows, with milestones marking key interactions
Imagine sticky notes covering a wall, organized into clusters like a collage - each cluster represents a theme
Think of a character sheet from a video game - stats, backstory, motivations, and goals all defined
Imagine being a fly on the wall in someone's office, silently watching how they work and noting everything

analogies.analogy: Like creating a character for a movie - you define their background, motivations, and personality to make decisions that resonate with your audience
Like an architect meeting with clients before designing a house - understanding needs, budget, and constraints before creating blueprints
Like a movie director mapping out every scene from opening to closing credits to ensure emotional impact at right moments
Like organizing a library where each person decides their own system - revealing how different minds categorize information differently
Like a journalist going undercover to understand a community from the inside - observing real behavior in natural settings
